operations.problem: The question states that a GNSS-equipped aircraft may be cleared below the MEA but not below the MOCA without mentioning the mandatory 300-foot controlled airspace floor prerequisite. A student could infer that GNSS equipment alone authorizes a below-MEA clearance, which is incomplete per the source.
operations.source_basis: Para 4-5-6a requires the assigned altitude to be at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace AND one of the listed conditions (including GNSS equipment) to clear an aircraft below the MEA but not below the MOCA.

match.question_text: A GNSS-equipped aircraft may be cleared below the MEA but not below the MOCA on the route segment being flown.

replacements.question_text: A controller may clear an aircraft below the MEA but not below the MOCA only when the assigned altitude is at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace and at least one of the listed conditions is met.
replacements.explanation: The rule requires both that the assigned altitude be at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace and that one of the three listed conditions (NAVAID proximity within 22 miles, radar procedures with MVA or MIA and lost communications instructions, or GNSS equipment) is satisfied.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The explanation only confirms the answer ('Yes. That is one of the jet-route exceptions.') without restating the controlling provision or teaching the applicable rule. A student learns which paragraph the exception belongs to but not what the exception requires.
operations.source_basis: Para 4-5-6b permits an aircraft on a jet route to be cleared below the MEA (but not below the prescribed minimum altitude for IFR operations) or above the maximum authorized altitude when radar service is provided.

match.question_text: On a jet route, may an aircraft be cleared below the MEA if radar service is provided and the clearance does not go below the prescribed minimum altitude for IFR operations?

replacements.question_text: On a jet route, may an aircraft be cleared below the MEA if radar service is provided and the clearance does not go below the prescribed minimum altitude for IFR operations?
replacements.explanation: An aircraft operating on a jet route may be cleared below the MEA, but not below the prescribed minimum altitude for IFR operations, or above the maximum authorized altitude when radar service is provided in either case.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The question asks which condition 'alone' allows below-MEA but not below-MOCA operation. No single condition alone authorizes this clearance; the source requires both the 300-foot controlled airspace floor prerequisite AND one of the listed conditions. The word 'alone' actively misrepresents the rule structure and could lead to operational misunderstanding.
operations.source_basis: Para 4-5-6a requires both that the assigned altitude be at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace and that one of the listed conditions is met. GNSS equipment is condition 3 of the three listed conditions.

content.instruction: Which condition allows below-MEA operation here?
content.question_text: Which condition alone allows below-MEA but not below-MOCA operation?

choices.text: The aircraft is GNSS equipped.
The pilot promises to climb if coverage becomes weak.
The aircraft is on a one-way route.
The route segment contains an unpublished fix.

content.explanation: GNSS equipment is one of the listed conditions for clearing below the MEA but not below the MOCA.

content.instruction: Which condition is listed for below-MEA operation?
content.question_text: Which of the following is a listed condition for clearing an aircraft below the MEA but not below the MOCA?

choices.text: The aircraft is GNSS equipped.
The pilot promises to climb if coverage becomes weak.
The aircraft is on a one-way route.
The route segment contains an unpublished fix.

content.explanation: GNSS equipment is one of the three listed conditions. The assigned altitude must also be at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace for any below-MEA but not below-MOCA clearance.

operations.problem: The front asks what equipment 'alone' satisfies a below-MEA exception, misrepresenting the compound rule. The back is thin ('GNSS equipment.') with no context about the larger rule framework, leaving the card insufficiently self-contained for accurate recall.
operations.source_basis: Para 4-5-6a lists three conditions for below-MEA but not below-MOCA operation, all subject to the prerequisite of at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace. GNSS equipment is condition 3.

match.front: What equipment alone can satisfy one of the below-MEA exceptions?

replacements.front: What equipment is listed as a condition for below-MEA but not below-MOCA clearance?
replacements.back: GNSS equipment. The altitude must also be at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace.

operations.problem: The explanation restates the answer without teaching the controlling operational rule. It says 'The correct restatement is climb via SID' but does not explain why this phraseology is used or what it accomplishes.
operations.source_basis: Subparagraph 4-5-7h10: 'When an aircraft has been issued an interim altitude and after departure ATC can subsequently clear the aircraft to climb to the original top altitude published in a SID that contains published crossing restrictions, instruct aircraft to climb via SID.' Note (block 52): 'Once re-issued the climb via clearance the interim altitude is canceled aircraft will continue climb to FL230 while complying with published restrictions.'

match.question_text: After an interim altitude on a SID, if ATC can now clear the aircraft to the originally published top altitude, what instruction is used?

replacements.question_text: After an interim altitude on a SID, if ATC can now clear the aircraft to the originally published top altitude, what instruction is used?
replacements.explanation: When an aircraft had an interim altitude on a SID with published crossing restrictions, the controller instructs 'climb via SID' to return the aircraft to the original published top altitude. Issuing 'climb via SID' cancels the interim altitude and authorizes climb to the published top altitude while the aircraft continues to comply with all published restrictions.

choices.text: Climb via SID.
Climb and maintain the top altitude.
Resume own navigation and maintain present altitude.
Proceed direct the first fix, maintain present altitude.

operations.problem: The correct answer is substantially longer (8 words) than each distractor (3-5 words), creating an answer-length cue that reveals the correct choice without operational knowledge. The explanation also restates the answer with no source rule.
operations.source_basis: Subparagraph 4-5-7h3: 'When cleared direct to a waypoint/fix without a published altitude, assign a crossing altitude.' Phraseology block 28: 'PROCEED DIRECT (fix/waypoint), CROSS (waypoint/fix) at (altitude) THEN DESCEND VIA (STAR name and number).'

match.question_text: If an aircraft is cleared direct to a waypoint without a published altitude on the STAR, what must be issued before descend via?

replacements.question_text: If an aircraft is cleared direct to a waypoint without a published altitude on the STAR, what must be issued before descend via?
replacements.explanation: When cleared direct to a waypoint or fix without a published altitude on the STAR, the controller must assign a crossing altitude. The correct phraseology is 'PROCEED DIRECT (fix), CROSS (fix) at (altitude), THEN DESCEND VIA (STAR).'

choices.text: Assign a crossing altitude at that waypoint or fix.
Cancel all altitude restrictions on the STAR before issuing descend via.
Issue a block altitude clearance for the remainder of the route.
Issue descend via without additional restrictions; the pilot determines the altitude.

operations.problem: The explanation only restates the answer: 'The controller must identify the new altitude as an amended altitude.' It does not teach why the amended-altitude notification is required or what operational change it signals.
operations.source_basis: Subparagraph 4-5-7f: 'When the pilot's discretion portion of a climb/descent clearance is being canceled by assigning a new altitude, inform the pilot that the new altitude is an amended altitude.' Example block 25: 'American Eighty Three, amend altitude, descend and maintain Flight Level two six zero.' Note block 26: 'American Eighty Three, at FL 280, has been cleared to descend at pilot's discretion to FL 240. Subsequently, the altitude assignment is changed to FL 260. Therefore, pilot's discretion is no longer authorized.'

match.question_text: When canceling a pilot's-discretion portion of a climb or descent by assigning a new altitude, what must the controller tell the pilot?

replacements.question_text: When canceling a pilot's-discretion portion of a climb or descent by assigning a new altitude, what must the controller tell the pilot?
replacements.explanation: When the pilot's-discretion portion of a climb or descent is canceled by assigning a new altitude, the controller must inform the pilot that the new altitude is an 'amended altitude.' The phraseology uses 'amend altitude' to signal that the previously authorized pilot's discretion is no longer in effect.

choices.text: That the new altitude is an amended altitude.
That the clearance is advisory only.
That no readback is required.
That speed restrictions are canceled.

operations.problem: The correct answer is 16 words while each distractor is 4-11 words, creating an answer-length cue. The explanation states the crossing altitude preserves IFR obstruction clearance but does not explain the operational condition (unpublished route means pilot lacks altitude information).
operations.source_basis: Subparagraph 4-5-7a: 'When issuing cruise in conjunction with an airport clearance limit and an unpublished route will be used, issue an appropriate crossing altitude to ensure terrain clearance until the aircraft reaches a fix, point, or route where the altitude information is available to the pilot.' Note 1 (block 3): 'The crossing altitude must assure IFR obstruction clearance to the point where the aircraft is established on a segment of a published route or instrument approach procedure.'

match.question_text: When issuing cruise with an airport clearance limit and an unpublished route, what is normally required?

replacements.question_text: When issuing cruise with an airport clearance limit and an unpublished route, what is normally required?
replacements.explanation: An appropriate crossing altitude is required when issuing cruise with an airport clearance limit and an unpublished route. This ensures IFR obstruction clearance until the aircraft reaches a fix, point, or route where the pilot has usable altitude information. The exception is when the destination airport has no published instrument approach procedure — then a cruise clearance without a crossing restriction may be issued.

choices.text: An appropriate crossing altitude to ensure IFR obstruction clearance.
Only a block altitude assigned for the entire route segment.
A speed restriction with no altitude requirement.
No additional restriction if the aircraft is above 10,000 feet.

operations.problem: The correct answer is 17 words while distractors are 5-6 words, making the answer identifiable by length alone. The explanation restates the answer without teaching the exception conditions from the source.
operations.source_basis: Exception block 4: 'in radar contact, you may specify an elapsed time interval restriction, in full minute increments only, without any reference to the UTC clock. The time restriction begins once the clearance has been acknowledged by the pilot.'

match.question_text: When may you use an elapsed-time climb or descent restriction without reference to the UTC clock?

replacements.question_text: When may you use an elapsed-time climb or descent restriction without reference to the UTC clock?
replacements.explanation: Elapsed-time restrictions without UTC reference are permitted only when the aircraft is in radar contact with direct controller-to-pilot two-way VHF/UHF voice communication. The restriction must be in full-minute increments and begins once the pilot acknowledges the clearance.

choices.text: In radar contact with direct two-way VHF/UHF voice communication.
Any time the pilot requests an elapsed-time restriction.
Only when relaying through an FSS or communications provider.
Only after the aircraft departs the published STAR procedure.

operations.problem: The explanation is vague: 'That is how the pilot's-discretion authority works with a crossing restriction.' It does not cite the source rule or state the operational consequence.
operations.source_basis: Note 20 block: '1. A descent clearance which specifies a crossing altitude authorizes descent at pilot's discretion for that portion of the flight to which the crossing altitude restriction applies. 2. Any other time that authorization to descend at pilot's discretion is intended, it must be specifically stated by the controller.'

match.question_text: A descent clearance with a crossing altitude authorizes descent at pilot's discretion for the portion of the flight to which the crossing restriction applies.

replacements.question_text: A descent clearance with a crossing altitude authorizes descent at pilot's discretion for the portion of the flight to which the crossing restriction applies.
replacements.explanation: A descent clearance specifying a crossing altitude authorizes pilot's-discretion descent for that portion of the flight to which the crossing restriction applies. At any other time, pilot's discretion must be specifically stated by the controller.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: This activity tests the same concept (cruise clearance crossing altitude for unpublished route) as question 3f9062d7 and flashcard 38a95a9a, all in multiple-choice recognition mode. The correct answer is also substantially longer than distractors.
operations.source_basis: Subparagraph 4-5-7h5: 'When vectoring or approving an aircraft to deviate, state an altitude to maintain and advise the pilot if you intend on clearing the aircraft to resume the climb via or descend via procedure.' Block 39 phraseology: 'DEVIATION (restrictions as necessary) APPROVED, MAINTAIN (altitude), EXPECT TO RESUME STAR/SID AT (waypoint/fix).' Block 38 note: 'Once an aircraft is established on a SID or STAR and is climbing or descending via, if the aircraft is cleared direct to a downstream fix, or cleared to deviate, the aircraft is considered to be off the procedure.'

content.question_text: When issuing cruise with an airport clearance limit and an unpublished route, what is normally required?

choices.text: An appropriate crossing altitude that ensures IFR obstruction clearance until the aircraft reaches a fix, point, or route where altitude information is available.
No crossing altitude if the aircraft is above 10,000 feet.
Only a speed restriction.
A block altitude instead of a crossing altitude.

content.explanation: That crossing altitude preserves IFR obstruction clearance until the aircraft reaches an area where the pilot has usable altitude information.

content.question_text: When an aircraft on a STAR descending via is approved to deviate, what must the controller state?

choices.text: An altitude to maintain and whether to expect to resume the STAR, along with any necessary restrictions.
Only the deviation approval with no further instructions.
Cancel the STAR and issue vectors to the next fix.
Instruct the pilot to report clear of weather before resuming.

content.explanation: When approving a deviation, the controller must state an altitude to maintain and advise whether to expect to resume the procedure. The phraseology is: DEVIATION (restrictions as necessary) APPROVED, MAINTAIN (altitude), EXPECT TO RESUME STAR/SID AT (waypoint/fix). The aircraft is considered off the procedure once cleared to deviate.

operations.problem: This list_membership activity tests the same concept (direct to waypoint without published altitude on STAR) as question 65de214b and flashcard 9be03209, all in recognition mode. The list_membership type is also misapplied — the activity functions as a standard multiple-choice question, not a list-membership classification.
operations.source_basis: Subparagraph 4-5-7i: 'When a pilot is unable to accept a clearance, issue revised instructions to ensure positive control and approved separation.' Note block 63: 'A pilot is therefore expected to advise the controller if a clearance cannot be accepted when the clearance is issued. We will try and other such acknowledgements do not constitute pilot acceptance of an ATC clearance. Expedite is not to be used in lieu of appropriate restrictions to ensure separation.'

content.question_text: Which use of descend-via phraseology is correct when clearing direct to a waypoint without a published altitude on the STAR?

choices.text: Assign a crossing altitude at that fix, then issue the descend-via clearance.
Use descend-via immediately with no altitude assignment because the pilot will choose one.
Cancel all published restrictions and issue only a speed restriction.
Use descend-via only after the aircraft reaches the runway transition.

content.explanation: If you clear direct to a waypoint without a published altitude, you must assign a crossing altitude before using descend via.

content.question_text: When a pilot cannot accept an ATC clearance, the controller must issue revised instructions to ensure positive control and approved separation. A response of 'we will try' constitutes pilot acceptance. The term 'expedite' may be substituted for appropriate restrictions to ensure separation.

choices.text: False
True

content.explanation: When a pilot cannot accept a clearance, the controller must issue revised instructions to ensure positive control and approved separation. The response 'we will try' does not constitute pilot acceptance, and the term 'expedite' is not to be used in lieu of appropriate restrictions. The statement is false because it contains two errors.

operations.problem: This situation_action activity tests the same concept (interim altitude then climb via SID to published top altitude) as question 078ebe57 and flashcard d6a57e9a, creating a three-way duplication of one operational rule in the same retrieval mode (recognition).
operations.source_basis: Note 61 block: 'Considering the principle that the last ATC clearance issued has precedence over the previous, the phraseology maintain (altitude) alone cancels previously issued altitude restrictions, including SID/STAR altitude restrictions unless they are restated or modified, and authorizes an unrestricted climb or descent. Speed restrictions remain in effect unless the controller explicitly cancels the speed restrictions.'

content.situation: An aircraft was climbing via a SID to an interim altitude of 10,000 feet. After departure, you can now clear it to the originally published top altitude on that SID.
content.para_context: When an aircraft has an interim altitude and can later be returned to the original top altitude on a SID with published crossing restrictions, the clearance is restated as climb via SID.

choices.text: Climb via SID.
Resume own navigation, maintain one zero thousand.
Climb and maintain the published top altitude.
Proceed direct the first fix and maintain one zero thousand.

content.explanation: If ATC can subsequently clear the aircraft to the original top altitude on the SID, the correct restatement is "climb via SID."

content.question_text: An aircraft on a STAR is maintaining an assigned altitude while awaiting descend-via clearance. The controller issues 'maintain five thousand.' What is the effect on the previously published STAR altitude restrictions?

choices.text: The STAR altitude restrictions are canceled unless restated or modified, and an unrestricted descent to 5,000 is authorized.
The published restrictions remain in full effect and the aircraft must still comply with them.
Only the speed restrictions are canceled; altitude restrictions remain.
The clearance is ambiguous and the pilot must request clarification.

content.explanation: The phraseology 'maintain (altitude)' alone cancels previously issued altitude restrictions, including SID/STAR altitude restrictions unless restated or modified, and authorizes an unrestricted climb or descent. Speed restrictions remain in effect unless explicitly canceled.

operations.problem: The front wording 'What does a crossing altitude do to a descent at pilot's discretion?' uses imprecise language — 'do to' is not the right way to ask about an operational effect. The front should ask about the relationship cleanly.
operations.source_basis: Note 20 block: 'A descent clearance which specifies a crossing altitude authorizes descent at pilot's discretion for that portion of the flight to which the crossing altitude restriction applies.'

match.front: What does a crossing altitude do to a descent at pilot's discretion?
match.back: It authorizes descent at pilot's discretion for the portion of flight to which the crossing restriction applies.

replacements.front: What effect does a crossing altitude have on descent at pilot's discretion?
replacements.back: It authorizes descent at pilot's discretion for the portion of flight to which the crossing restriction applies.

operations.problem: The front 'When may a cruise clearance to an airport without a published instrument approach be issued without a crossing restriction?' contains the answer ('without a published instrument approach') in the question itself. The back merely repeats the condition from the front, making this a non-retrieval card.
operations.source_basis: Subparagraph 4-5-7a: 'When issuing a cruise clearance to an airport which does not have a published instrument approach, a cruise clearance without a crossing restriction may be issued.' Note 2 (block 3): 'Under those conditions, a cruise clearance without a crossing restriction authorizes a pilot to determine the minimum IFR altitude as prescribed in 14 CFR section 91.177 and descend to it at pilot discretion if it is lower than the altitude specified in the cruise clearance.'

match.front: When may a cruise clearance to an airport without a published instrument approach be issued without a crossing restriction?
match.back: When the airport has no published instrument approach procedure.

replacements.front: Under what condition may a cruise clearance be issued without a crossing restriction?
replacements.back: When the destination airport has no published instrument approach procedure.

operations.problem: This card tests the same concept (interim altitude then climb via SID to published top altitude) as question 078ebe57 and activity 02de8708. Three items testing one subparagraph rule in recognition/fill-in mode is excessive. The back is also very thin for a phraseology card.
operations.source_basis: Subparagraph 4-5-7e: 'When a portion of a climb/descent may be authorized at the pilot's discretion, specify the altitude the aircraft must climb/descend to followed by the altitude to maintain at the pilot's discretion.' Phraseology block 22: 'CLIMB/DESCEND NOW TO (altitude), THEN CLIMB/DESCEND AT PILOT'S DISCRETION MAINTAIN (altitude).'

match.front: After an interim altitude on a SID, what instruction is used when ATC can clear the aircraft back to the published top altitude?

replacements.front: When authorizing only a portion of a climb or descent at pilot's discretion, what must the controller specify?
replacements.back: The altitude to climb/descend to first, followed by the altitude to maintain at pilot's discretion. Phraseology: CLIMB/DESCEND NOW TO (altitude), THEN CLIMB/DESCEND AT PILOT'S DISCRETION MAINTAIN (altitude).

operations.problem: The back is imprecise: 'or the equivalent descend-via restatement' is not a phraseology defined in the source. The phraseology blocks provide only the climb-via form for restatement after direct routing. The card should state the known phraseology and note the rule about the name not needing to be restated.
operations.source_basis: Interpretation block 36: 'After a climb via or descend via clearance has been issued, if the aircraft is cleared direct to a waypoint/fix, the climb via or descend via clearance must be restated. The name of the SID or STAR does not need to be restated.' Phraseology block 37: 'CLEARED/PROCEED DIRECT (waypoint/fix), THEN CLIMB VIA SID.'

match.front: What phraseology is used when a climb-via or descend-via clearance must be restated after clearing the aircraft direct to a waypoint or fix?
match.back: Cleared or proceed direct (waypoint or fix), then climb via SID, or the equivalent descend-via restatement.

replacements.front: What phraseology restates a climb-via clearance after an aircraft is cleared direct to a waypoint?
replacements.back: CLEARED/PROCEED DIRECT (waypoint/fix), THEN CLIMB VIA SID. The SID/STAR name does not need to be restated.

operations.problem: The card_type is 'phraseology' but the back is a prose description ('Identify the new altitude as an amended altitude'), not the actual phraseology. A phraseology card must present the phraseology template the controller would use.
operations.source_basis: Subparagraph 4-5-7f: 'When the pilot's discretion portion of a climb/descent clearance is being canceled by assigning a new altitude, inform the pilot that the new altitude is an amended altitude.' Example block 25: 'American Eighty Three, amend altitude, descend and maintain Flight Level two six zero.'

match.front: When canceling a pilot's-discretion portion by assigning a new altitude, what must the controller say?
match.back: Identify the new altitude as an amended altitude.

replacements.front: What phraseology cancels the pilot's-discretion portion of a climb or descent by assigning a new altitude?
replacements.back: AMEND ALTITUDE, (climb/descend) AND MAINTAIN (altitude).

operations.problem: The correct answer text is substantially longer than all three distractors, creating an answer-length cue that reveals the answer regardless of content knowledge. The distractors are brief, single-concept phrases while the correct answer is a compound procedural statement. The explanation merely restates the answer instead of teaching the controlling rule and its phraseology options.
operations.source_basis: FAA JO 7110.65, Para 4-5-8 body: 'If practicable, inform an aircraft when to expect climb or descent clearance or to request altitude change from another facility.' Phraseology: 'EXPECT HIGHER/LOWER IN (number of miles or minutes) MILES/MINUTES, or AT (fix). REQUEST ALTITUDE/FLIGHT LEVEL CHANGE FROM (name of facility).'

match.question_text: If practicable, what should be told to an aircraft that cannot yet be climbed or descended?

replacements.question_text: According to FAA JO 7110.65, what information should a controller provide to an aircraft, if practicable, when a climb or descent clearance cannot yet be issued?
replacements.explanation: When a climb or descent clearance cannot yet be issued, the controller should inform the aircraft, if practicable, when to expect the clearance or when to request an altitude change from another facility. The prescribed phraseology offers two patterns: 'EXPECT HIGHER/LOWER IN (miles or minutes) MILES/MINUTES' or 'AT (fix),' and 'REQUEST ALTITUDE/FLIGHT LEVEL CHANGE FROM (facility).' If required, the advisory may specify a time, fix, or altitude.

choices.text: The estimated time of the next clearance and the frequency of the following sector.
When to expect a climb or descent, or when to request the altitude change from another facility.
The current weather at the destination and the aircraft's present position relative to the next fix.
A holding instruction with the reason the altitude change is not yet available.

operations.problem: The distractors are not all operationally plausible: 'Climb via SID now' belongs to the departure phase and would not be confused with an en-route altitude anticipation advisory. The explanation restates the correct answer without teaching the controlling procedure — it says only 'The correct advisory is to tell the aircraft to expect higher in 20 miles.'
operations.source_basis: FAA JO 7110.65, Para 4-5-8 body: 'If practicable, inform an aircraft when to expect climb or descent clearance.' Phraseology: 'EXPECT HIGHER/LOWER IN (number of miles or minutes) MILES/MINUTES.' The controller must specify the direction (higher or lower) and the distance in miles or time in minutes.

replacements.explanation: When a climb or descent clearance cannot yet be issued, the controller should inform the aircraft, if practicable, when to expect the clearance. The prescribed phraseology is 'EXPECT HIGHER/LOWER IN (number of miles or minutes) MILES/MINUTES.' The advisory must specify the correct direction and match the expected distance in miles or time in minutes to the scenario.

choices.text: EXPECT HIGHER IN TWO ZERO MILES.
MAINTAIN PRESENT ALTITUDE.
DESCEND AT PILOT'S DISCRETION.
VERIFY YOUR ASSIGNED ALTITUDE.

operations.problem: The stem asks 'Which advisory fits this paragraph?' — a document-structure question that tests recognition of which option belongs to Paragraph 4-5-8 rather than the controller's operational decision of which advisory to issue in a given situation. operations.source_basis: FAA JO 7110.65, Para 4-5-8 phraseology: 'REQUEST ALTITUDE/FLIGHT LEVEL CHANGE FROM (name of facility). If required, AT (time, fix, or altitude).' This is one of two advisory options available when a climb or descent clearance cannot yet be issued.

replacements.question_text: An aircraft will be handed off to Kansas City Center after crossing the BUM fix. The current controller cannot issue a climb clearance, and the aircraft should request the altitude change from the next facility. What phraseology should the controller use?
replacements.explanation: When a climb or descent clearance cannot yet be issued and the aircraft should request the altitude change from another facility, the controller uses the phraseology 'REQUEST ALTITUDE/FLIGHT LEVEL CHANGE FROM (name of facility).' When required, the advisory may include the location where to make the request, such as 'AT BUM.'

choices.text: REQUEST ALTITUDE CHANGE FROM KANSAS CITY CENTER AT BUM.
EXPECT HIGHER AFTER BUM.
CONTACT KANSAS CITY CENTER ON ARRIVAL.

operations.problem: The front references 'this paragraph' as a generic document anchor rather than an ATC concept, making the card a paragraph-location recall task. The source states only the duty and the phraseology; it does not explain why the procedure is useful.
operations.source_basis: FAA JO 7110.65, Para 4-5-8 body: 'If practicable, inform an aircraft when to expect climb or descent clearance or to request altitude change from another facility.' Phraseology: 'EXPECT HIGHER/LOWER IN (number of miles or minutes) MILES/MINUTES, or AT (fix). REQUEST ALTITUDE/FLIGHT LEVEL CHANGE FROM (name of facility). If required, AT (time, fix, or altitude).' operations.problem: The explanation restates the answer ('They must all be reconfirmed') without teaching the rule or placing it in context. match.question_text: In USA operations, what must be done with all pilot altitude readbacks?

replacements.question_text: In USA operations, what must be done with all pilot altitude readbacks?
replacements.explanation: USA controllers must reconfirm every pilot altitude readback. Unlike the initial-contact altitude-verification rule — which has three stated exceptions — this USA requirement applies to all pilot altitude readbacks without distinction.

choices.text: Reconfirm them.
Log them only if they are incorrect.
Ignore them if the aircraft is radar identified.
Repeat them only on first contact.

match.question_text: When should altitude confirmation normally be requested in nonradar operations?

replacements.question_text: When should altitude confirmation normally be requested in nonradar operations?
replacements.explanation: Nonradar altitude confirmation is normally required on initial contact and when position reports are received. The paragraph lists three exceptions: the pilot states the assigned altitude; a new altitude is assigned to a climbing or descending aircraft; or (TERMINAL) an intrafacility transfer occurs.

choices.text: On initial contact and when position reports are received, unless an exception applies.
Only after an interfacility handoff from another ATC facility.
Only when the aircraft is climbing or descending through an assigned altitude.
Only for aircraft operating at or above the lowest usable flight level.

operations.problem: The explanation ('That case uses verify assigned flight level') restates the answer without teaching when each phraseology variant applies. The source distinguishes three phraseology regimes based on aircraft state and altitude — the explanation should teach that distinction.

replacements.question_text: Which phraseology fits a flight-level assignment at or above the lowest usable flight level?
replacements.explanation: For aircraft assigned a flight level at or above the lowest usable flight level, the controller uses 'VERIFY ASSIGNED FLIGHT LEVEL' followed by the flight level. Below the lowest usable flight level, the controller uses 'VERIFY ASSIGNED ALTITUDE' instead. In level flight, the simpler 'VERIFY AT' is used.

choices.text: Verify assigned flight level three three zero.
Verify assigned altitude three three zero.
Affirmative flight level three three zero.
Negative maintain flight level three three zero.

content.situation: No delay is expected. The aircraft was previously cleared via J174 to its current clearance limit, but the new clearance limit lies on J175, to which J174 does not extend. The previously issued routing is not valid to the new fix.
content.para_context: When issuing a clearance beyond a clearance limit, specify the route using complete details or the phrase 'via last routing cleared' only when the most recently issued routing to the new clearance limit remains valid and verbiage will be reduced.

choices.text: Issue complete details of the new route, including the airway and any relevant fixes.
Use the phrase 'via last routing cleared' because no delay is expected and the aircraft was previously on an assigned route.
Omit the route description entirely and issue only the new clearance limit and the assigned altitude.

content.explanation: The phrase 'via last routing cleared' may only be used when the most recently issued routing to the new clearance limit remains valid. When the routing has changed — for example, the new fix requires a different airway — the controller must issue complete details of the new route.

operations.source_basis: 4-6-2 b.2.(b) The phrase 'via last routing cleared.' Use this phrase only when the most recently issued routing to the new clearance limit is valid and verbiage will be reduced.
