match.question_text: When may a maximum holding airspeed advisory be required?

replacements.question_text: When may a maximum holding airspeed advisory be required?
replacements.explanation: The advisory is required when the aircraft is approved for a higher speed and the holding pattern protects that speed, when the aircraft is observed deviating from holding airspace, or when it is cleared into an airspeed-restricted holding pattern whose icon has not been published.

choices.text: When an aircraft is approved to exceed the maximum airspeed and is cleared into a pattern that protects the greater speed.
Whenever a controller issues any holding clearance, regardless of airspeed or pattern protection.
Only when the pilot has specifically asked the controller to state the maximum holding airspeed for the pattern.
When an aircraft is observed at an altitude that differs from the published holding pattern altitude.

operations.problem: Answer length cue: the correct answer lists three conditions in approximately 23 words while distractors are terse 4-8 word 'Only for...' statements. The three conditions are necessary for a complete answer, so the correct choice cannot be shortened; distractors must be expanded to comparable length and specificity.
operations.source_basis: Source block sequence 1 (note) item e: Direction of holding pattern turns only if left turns are to be made, the pilot requests it, or you consider it necessary.

match.question_text: When must turn direction be specified in a holding clearance?

replacements.question_text: When must turn direction be specified in a holding clearance?
replacements.explanation: Direction of holding pattern turns is specified only when left turns are to be made, when the pilot requests it, or when the controller considers it necessary.

choices.text: When left turns are to be made, when the pilot requests it, or when the controller considers it necessary.
In every holding clearance issued to any aircraft, regardless of whether right or left turns will be flown.
Only when the holding pattern is established at a fix where DME or RNAV leg lengths are specified in minutes.
Only when the published holding pattern includes a non-standard holding speed restriction.

operations.problem: Implausible distractors (destination gate number, reason for the delay, approach light intensity setting) do not represent operational misunderstandings an ATC trainee would hold. Combined with an answer length cue, the question fails to discriminate knowledge effectively. The distractors should reflect adjacent operational concepts a trainee might plausibly confuse with holding instruction elements.
operations.source_basis: Source block sequence 0 (body): When issuing holding instructions, specify: a. Direction of holding from the fix/waypoint. b. Holding fix or waypoint.

match.question_text: Which item belongs in a holding clearance?

replacements.question_text: Which of the following must be specified when issuing holding instructions?
replacements.explanation: When issuing holding instructions the controller must specify the direction of holding from the fix or waypoint and the holding fix or waypoint. The holding radial or course is also specified, while leg length, turn direction, and airspeed advisories are conditional.

choices.text: Direction of holding from the fix or waypoint.
The altitude to maintain while holding at the fix.
The reason the aircraft is being placed in the holding pattern.
The expected arrival time at the clearance limit.

operations.problem: Near-duplicate of question 7140acc1: both test the identical concept (direction of holding from the fix) with nearly identical weak distractors and the same answer length cue. The activity adds no retrieval diversity. Replaced with a knowledge check testing condition 2 of the maximum holding airspeed advisory rule, which is not separately tested elsewhere in the shard.
operations.source_basis: Source block sequence 2 (phraseology) item f.2: Issue maximum holding airspeed advisories when an aircraft is observed deviating from the holding pattern airspace area.

content.question_text: Which item is part of complete holding instructions?

choices.text: The direction of holding from the fix or waypoint.
The airport's ATIS code.
The destination gate assignment.
A reason for every prior vector.

content.explanation: Direction from the fix, the fix itself, and the holding course or route are part of the required holding instructions.

content.question_text: A controller observes an aircraft deviating from the holding pattern airspace area. What action is required?

choices.text: Issue the maximum holding airspeed advisory for the holding pattern airspace area.
Instruct the aircraft to exit the holding pattern immediately and proceed direct to the clearance limit.
No action is necessary; the advisory is only triggered by pilot request or published speed restrictions.
Issue a new holding clearance with a different holding fix farther from the deviated airspace.

content.explanation: When an aircraft is observed deviating from the holding pattern airspace area, the controller must issue the maximum holding airspeed advisory. This is one of three conditions requiring the advisory per the holding instructions rule.

operations.problem: The activity recognizes only one of three valid conditions for specifying turn direction. The source lists three triggers: left turns, pilot request, and controller judgment. The activity marks only 'when left turns are to be made' as correct, implying the other two conditions do not require turn direction specification. This misrepresents the source rule and would teach a trainee that pilot-requested or controller-determined turn direction need not be specified.
operations.source_basis: Source block sequence 1 (note) item e: Direction of holding pattern turns only if left turns are to be made, the pilot requests it, or you consider it necessary.

content.instruction: When must turn direction be stated?
content.question_text: Which case requires you to specify the direction of turns in the holding pattern?

choices.text: When left turns are to be made.
In every holding clearance, even for standard right turns.
Only when the aircraft is above FL180.
Only when the hold is charted.

content.explanation: Turn direction is specified only for left turns, on pilot request, or when the controller considers it necessary.

content.question_text: In which of the following cases must turn direction be specified in a holding clearance?

choices.text: When the pilot requests the turn direction.
In every holding clearance without exception.
Only when the holding fix is a VOR rather than an RNAV waypoint.
Only when the aircraft is holding below 10,000 feet MSL.

content.explanation: Turn direction must be specified in the holding clearance when left turns are to be made, when the pilot requests it, or when the controller considers it necessary. Right turns are standard and need not be stated.

operations.problem: Condition 3 on the card back is incomplete. The source states the advisory is required when the aircraft is 'Cleared into an airspeed restricted holding pattern in which the icon has not been published.' The card condenses this to 'when the airspeed-restricted holding icon has not been published,' omitting the operational context that the aircraft must be cleared into such a pattern. The icon being unpublished alone is not sufficient to trigger the advisory.
operations.source_basis: Source block sequence 2 (phraseology) item f.3: Cleared into an airspeed restricted holding pattern in which the icon has not been published.

match.front: When is a maximum holding airspeed advisory required?
match.back: When the pattern protects a higher approved speed, when the aircraft is observed deviating from holding airspace, or when the airspeed-restricted holding icon has not been published.

replacements.front: When is a maximum holding airspeed advisory required?
replacements.back: When the aircraft is approved for a higher speed and the pattern protects it, when the aircraft is observed deviating from holding airspace, or when the aircraft is cleared into an airspeed-restricted holding pattern whose icon has not been published.

operations.problem: The correct answer spans 20 words and quotes the source verbatim, while every distractor is a terse 7-to-11-word phrase. The length gap reveals the correct answer regardless of content knowledge, undermining the assessment's validity.
operations.source_basis: You may use as a holding fix a location which the pilot can determine by visual reference to the surface if he/she is familiar with it.

match.question_text: When may a visual holding point be used?

replacements.question_text: Under what condition may you assign a visual holding point to an aircraft?
replacements.explanation: A visual holding point requires the pilot to determine the location by visual reference to the surface and to be familiar with it. Both conditions must be present before the controller may assign the holding point.

choices.text: When the pilot can visually identify the location and is familiar with it.
When the pilot can navigate to it by GPS and holds an IFR flight plan.
When radar coverage is available and the point is visible on the controller display.
When the location is charted on a visual approach plate and is in Class B airspace.

operations.problem: Same answer-length cue as the parallel question: the correct choice in this knowledge_check is a 17-word verbatim source quote while the three distractors are each 6-to-9-word phrases, revealing the correct answer by inspection.
operations.source_basis: You may use as a holding fix a location which the pilot can determine by visual reference to the surface if he/she is familiar with it.

content.question_text: When may you use a visual holding point?

choices.text: When the pilot can determine the location by visual reference to the surface and is familiar with it.
Whenever the point is near the airport.
Only when radar service is unavailable.
Whenever the pilot requests any landmark.

content.explanation: A visual holding point may be used only if the pilot can determine it visually and is familiar with it.

content.question_text: Under what condition may you assign a visual holding point to an aircraft?

choices.text: When the pilot can visually identify the location and is familiar with it.
When the pilot can navigate to it by GPS and holds an IFR flight plan.
When radar coverage is available and the point is visible on the controller display.
When the location is charted on a visual approach plate and is in Class B airspace.

content.explanation: The controller may use a visual holding fix only when the pilot can determine the location by visual reference to the surface and is familiar with the location. Both conditions must be satisfied.

operations.problem: This flashcard asks the same retrieval target as flashcard 6712b10b: both cards test when a visual holding point may be used, with nearly identical backs. Flashcard 6712b10b frames the concept as 'What two conditions must both exist' which is a more structured and precise prompt than 'When may a visual holding point be used.'
operations.source_basis: Flashcard 6712b10b already covers the two-condition visual-holding-point requirement with a more specific front prompt and equivalent back content.

match.front: When may a visual holding point be used?
match.back: When the pilot can determine it by visual reference to the surface and is familiar with it.

operations.problem: The explanation restates the answer in five words without teaching the controller's evaluative responsibility. Additionally, the question is a near-duplicate of question b1b672c3 — both ask which conditions allow a deviation, differing only in phrasing ('When may…' versus 'Which factor pair controls…').
operations.source_basis: Approve a pilot's request to deviate from the prescribed holding flight path if obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

match.question_text: Which factor pair controls whether a holding-path deviation may be approved?

replacements.question_text: A pilot requests to deviate from the prescribed holding flight path. You check and confirm that both obstacles and traffic conditions permit the deviation. What is the correct action?
replacements.explanation: The controller must evaluate both obstacle clearance and traffic conditions before acting on a pilot's request to deviate from the prescribed holding flight path. When both factors permit the deviation, approve the request.

choices.text: Approve the deviation.
Deny the deviation because the regulation requires the prescribed path.
Approve the deviation only if the pilot declares an emergency.
Coordinate with the next sector before issuing an approval.

operations.problem: The instruction and question text both use the singular 'Which factor' while the correct answer names two distinct factors (obstacles and traffic conditions). This grammatical mismatch confuses the retrieval target. The list_membership type with a single multiple-choice question also functions identically to a knowledge_check.
operations.source_basis: Approve a pilot's request to deviate from the prescribed holding flight path if obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

content.question_text: Which factor must permit a holding-path deviation before ATC approves it?

choices.text: Obstacle and traffic conditions.
Only the pilot's fuel state.
Only the destination weather.
Only adjacent-facility workload.

content.explanation: The deviation is approved only if obstacle and traffic conditions permit.

content.question_text: Which factors must permit a holding-path deviation before ATC approves the request?

choices.text: Obstacle and traffic conditions.
Only the pilot's fuel state.
Only the destination weather.
Only adjacent-facility workload.

content.explanation: A pilot's request to deviate from the prescribed holding flight path may be approved only when both obstacle clearance and traffic conditions permit the deviation. Neither condition alone is sufficient for approval.

operations.problem: The explanation reads 'That is the full standard in this paragraph.' It references document structure rather than teaching the controller's evaluative responsibility, and it uses a generic paragraph reference that is meaningless to a student.
operations.source_basis: Approve a pilot's request to deviate from the prescribed holding flight path if obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

content.question_text: You may approve a pilot's request to deviate from the prescribed holding flight path if obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: That is the full standard in this paragraph.

content.question_text: You may approve a pilot's request to deviate from the prescribed holding flight path if obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: The controller must evaluate both obstacle clearance and traffic conditions before approving a holding-path deviation. When both conditions permit it, the pilot's request may be approved.

operations.problem: This flashcard duplicates flashcard 33e8daa9 within the same retrieval mode. Both cards ask when a holding-path deviation may be approved, retrieving the identical two-condition rule. Flashcard 33e8daa9 uses the more specific prompt 'What two factors control approval' which targets the conditions directly rather than the general 'When may' framing.
operations.source_basis: Flashcard 33e8daa9 already covers the obstacles-and-traffic-conditions requirement with a more targeted front prompt and equivalent back content.

match.front: When may a pilot's requested deviation from the prescribed holding flight path be approved?
match.back: When obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

operations.problem: The correct answer is significantly longer than all three distractors, creating a structural cue that reveals the correct choice regardless of content knowledge. The explanation merely restates the answer ('It must be separated from any aircraft occupying the course...') without teaching the controlling principle or connecting the answer to the source rule.
operations.source_basis: Separate an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID from any other aircraft occupying the course which the holding aircraft will follow if it does not receive signals from the NAVAID.

match.question_text: How must an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID be separated from other aircraft?

replacements.question_text: An aircraft is holding at an unmonitored NAVAID. From which traffic must it be separated?
replacements.explanation: An aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID must be separated from any other aircraft occupying the course the holding aircraft would follow if it loses the NAVAID signal.

choices.text: Only from aircraft on the same airway at the holding altitude.
From aircraft on the course it would follow if the NAVAID signal were lost.
Only after the pilot reports NAVAID signal loss on the frequency.
From all IFR traffic within the lateral confines of the holding pattern.

operations.problem: Tests the same concept as question 32375278: identifying which traffic must be separated from the holding aircraft. Both items ask essentially the same question with only superficial rewording of the stem. A single-source-sentence paragraph cannot support two questions on the same retrieval angle.
operations.source_basis: Separate an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID from any other aircraft occupying the course which the holding aircraft will follow if it does not receive signals from the NAVAID. Both this item and 32375278 test identification of the protected traffic u2014 the same core concept.

match.question_text: Which other aircraft must be considered for separation from the holding aircraft at an unmonitored NAVAID?

operations.problem: True/false restatement of the source sentence with an extremely thin explanation ('That is exactly how the protected course is defined'). The item adds no educational value beyond the multiple-choice questions that test the same rule with plausible distractors. It is the fourth question covering the same single sentence.
operations.source_basis: Separate an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID from any other aircraft occupying the course which the holding aircraft will follow if it does not receive signals from the NAVAID. This true/false item restates the rule verbatim without requiring operational application.

match.question_text: The required protection at an unmonitored NAVAID is based on the course the holding aircraft would follow if it stopped receiving the NAVAID signal.

operations.problem: Knowledge check activity is nearly identical to question 32375278 in both stem and answer. Both ask how the holding aircraft must be separated from other traffic, and both have the same correct answer and structurally similar distractors. Cross-format redundancy for a single-sentence source provides no additional learning value.
operations.source_basis: Separate an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID from any other aircraft occupying the course which the holding aircraft will follow if it does not receive signals from the NAVAID. This activity, question 32375278, and question a09cfde9 all test the same identification concept.

content.question_text: How must an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID be separated from other aircraft on the relevant course?

choices.text: Separate it from any aircraft occupying the course the holding aircraft would follow if it stopped receiving the NAVAID signal.
Separate it only from aircraft on final approach.
No extra separation is needed if the holding clearance was read back correctly.
Separate it only from aircraft above the holding altitude.

content.explanation: The protection is tied to the course the aircraft will follow if the unmonitored NAVAID signal is lost.

operations.problem: List membership activity tests the same 'which traffic' identification as question a09cfde9. The list_membership type is also a poor fit for a rule that identifies a single protected category rather than a genuine list. Duplicative within a paragraph that has only one operational concept.
operations.source_basis: Separate an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID from any other aircraft occupying the course which the holding aircraft will follow if it does not receive signals from the NAVAID. The source identifies one protected traffic category, not a list.

content.question_text: Which other aircraft must be considered for separation from the holding aircraft?

choices.text: Aircraft occupying the course the holding aircraft would follow after signal loss.
Traffic pattern aircraft near the airport.
Aircraft at the same altitude block regardless of the signal-loss course.
Aircraft receiving vectors from another controller.

content.explanation: The protected course is the one the holding aircraft would fly if it no longer received the NAVAID signal.

operations.problem: Requirement check true/false activity is redundant with the true/false question bc3bb225. Both present the same statement for verification with substantially the same explanation. Adds no distinct retrieval mode or operational angle.
operations.source_basis: Separate an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID from any other aircraft occupying the course which the holding aircraft will follow if it does not receive signals from the NAVAID. This activity and question bc3bb225 test the same true/false verification.

content.question_text: You must separate an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID from any other aircraft occupying the course it would follow if it stopped receiving signals from that NAVAID.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: That is the specific protection required for holds at unmonitored NAVAIDs.

operations.problem: The flashcard asks 'Why is extra protection required at an unmonitored NAVAID?' and answers 'Because the aircraft may continue along a predictable course if it loses the NAVAID signal while holding.' The source paragraph states only the separation requirement; it provides no rationale, reason, or explanation for why the protection is required. Teaching a fabricated rationale as fact misrepresents the source material.
operations.source_basis: The source states only: 'Separate an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID from any other aircraft occupying the course which the holding aircraft will follow if it does not receive signals from the NAVAID.' No rationale, purpose, or causal explanation appears anywhere in the paragraph or its surrounding section.

match.front: Why is extra protection required at an unmonitored NAVAID?
match.back: Because the aircraft may continue along a predictable course if it loses the NAVAID signal while holding.

operations.problem: Redundant with flashcards b2056118 and 855a3914. This card asks 'How is protection defined...' while b2056118 asks 'What traffic matters...' and 855a3914 asks 'What if another aircraft is about to enter...' u2014 all three elicit the same separation rule from the same source sentence with only phrasing differences. Two flashcards are sufficient for this paragraph.
operations.source_basis: Separate an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID from any other aircraft occupying the course which the holding aircraft will follow if it does not receive signals from the NAVAID. Flashcard b2056118 covers the protected traffic category and 855a3914 covers the controller action u2014 this card adds no distinct retrieval target.

match.front: How is protection defined for an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID?
match.back: Separate it from any aircraft occupying the course it would follow if it stopped receiving the NAVAID signal.

operations.problem: The correct answer is 24 words while every distractor is 9-10 words, creating a reliable answer-length cue. The 'tower is closed' distractor has no anchor in the paragraph's operational context and is implausible; a student can eliminate it on irrelevance alone.
operations.source_basis: Source body block: 'USAF. The holding restriction applies only when an arriving aircraft is between the ILS OM or the fix used in lieu of the OM and the runway.'

match.question_text: What additional limitation applies in the USAF note?

replacements.question_text: What limitation does the USAF note add to the ILS holding restriction?
replacements.explanation: The USAF note narrows the holding restriction: it applies only when an arriving aircraft is between the OM or substitute fix and the runway, not to all aircraft operating in that airspace.

choices.text: The restriction applies to all aircraft on or near the localizer, whether arriving or departing.
The restriction applies only to USAF military aircraft.
The restriction applies only when an arriving aircraft is between the OM or substitute fix and the runway.
The restriction applies only above 5,000 feet AGL.

operations.problem: The stem verbatim restates the source text as a statement and asks whether it is true. This is tautological — it tests recognition of the source text rather than operational understanding or rule application. The explanation adds nothing beyond the source statement itself.
operations.source_basis: Source body block: 'When the official weather observation indicates a ceiling of less than 800 feet or visibility of 2 miles, do not authorize aircraft to hold below 5,000 feet AGL inbound toward the airport on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer between the ILS OM or the fix used in lieu of the OM and the airport.'

match.question_text: When the weather trigger exists, aircraft must not be authorized to hold below 5,000 feet AGL inbound toward the airport on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer between the OM or substitute fix and the airport.

operations.problem: This knowledge_check activity tests the same concept (weather trigger) as question 5ac00fa5 in the same multiple-choice retrieval mode. The stems are near-identical — 'When does the ILS holding restriction apply?' vs 'What weather trigger activates the ILS holding restriction?' — and both present four-option multiple-choice selections about the ceiling/visibility trigger. The paragraph content is too narrow to justify two items in the same format on this one fact.
operations.source_basis: Source body block: 'When the official weather observation indicates a ceiling of less than 800 feet or visibility of 2 miles, do not authorize aircraft to hold...' Compare with question 5ac00fa5-cc6a-4ef3-93ed-8528c31ef62c in the same paragraph targets.

content.question_text: When does the ILS holding restriction apply?

choices.text: When the official weather observation shows a ceiling below 800 feet or visibility of 2 miles.
Only when both ceiling and visibility are below minimums at the same time.
Only when visibility is below 1 mile.
Whenever any ILS approach is in use.

content.explanation: The restriction is triggered by a ceiling of less than 800 feet or visibility of 2 miles.

operations.problem: This list_membership activity tests the same concept (prohibited holding position parameters) as question 37bf039c in the same multiple-choice retrieval mode. Both ask which holding situation violates the restriction and present structurally identical choices that each violate one element of the protected area.
operations.source_basis: Source body block: '...do not authorize aircraft to hold below 5,000 feet AGL inbound toward the airport on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer between the ILS OM or the fix used in lieu of the OM and the airport.' Compare with question 37bf039c-26f1-4336-b2bd-8ef7efc43bed in the same paragraph targets.

content.instruction: Which hold would violate the restriction?
content.question_text: Which holding situation falls inside the prohibited ILS protection area when the weather trigger exists?

choices.text: Holding at 4,500 feet AGL inbound on the localizer within 1 mile between the OM and the airport.
Holding 6,000 feet AGL on the localizer in the same segment.
Holding 4,500 feet AGL more than 3 miles outside the localizer corridor.
Holding 4,500 feet AGL outbound away from the airport outside the protected segment.

content.explanation: The prohibited case is below 5,000 feet AGL, inbound toward the airport, on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer, between the OM or substitute fix and the airport.

operations.problem: The question_text contains paragraph-location scaffolding ('the 4-6-8 weather trigger') which substitutes a document reference for operational substance. The explanation merely restates the answer without connecting it to the controlling operational principle, and the source_label repeats the paragraph number.
operations.source_basis: Source body block: 'When the official weather observation indicates a ceiling of less than 800 feet or visibility of 2 miles, do not authorize aircraft to hold below 5,000 feet AGL inbound toward the airport on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer between the ILS OM or the fix used in lieu of the OM and the airport.'

lookup_context: Holding would be below 5,000 feet AGL
Inbound toward airport on or within 1 statute mile of localizer between OM/substitute fix and airport
content.task: Decide whether holding may be authorized in the protected area.
content.instruction: Apply the ILS critical-area holding restriction.
content.question_text: When the 4-6-8 weather trigger exists, may ATC authorize holding below 5,000 feet AGL inbound toward the airport on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer between the OM/substitute fix and the airport?

choices.text: No, do not authorize that holding.
Yes, if the aircraft is above the glideslope.
Yes, if the pilot accepts responsibility.
Yes, because the restriction applies only below 3,000 feet AGL.

content.explanation: That is the protected area and altitude restriction for ILS critical-area holding.

lookup_context: Holding would be below 5,000 feet AGL
Inbound toward airport on or within 1 statute mile of localizer between OM/substitute fix and airport
content.task: Decide whether holding may be authorized in the protected area.
content.instruction: Apply the ILS critical-area holding restriction.
content.question_text: When the ILS protection weather trigger exists, may ATC authorize holding below 5,000 feet AGL inbound toward the airport on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer between the OM/substitute fix and the airport?

choices.text: No, do not authorize that holding.
Yes, if the aircraft is above the glideslope.
Yes, if the pilot accepts responsibility.
Yes, because the restriction applies only below 3,000 feet AGL.

content.explanation: When the weather trigger exists (ceiling less than 800 feet or visibility of 2 miles), the rule prohibits any hold within the protected ILS critical area: below 5,000 feet AGL, inbound toward the airport, on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer, between the OM/substitute fix and the airport. The described holding position falls entirely within that prohibited area.

operations.problem: The correct answer ('The STAR name and the transition, if necessary.') is 47 characters while the three distractors range from 20 to 26 characters. The nearly 2x length differential reveals the answer before the learner evaluates the content.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 4-7-1, block 1 (phraseology): 'The clearance must include the name and transition, if necessary, of the STAR/RNAV STAR to be flown.'

match.question_text: When a STAR or RNAV STAR is assigned, what must the clearance include?

replacements.question_text: When a STAR or RNAV STAR is assigned, what must the clearance include?
replacements.explanation: The clearance must include the name and transition, if necessary, of the STAR/RNAV STAR to be flown. The destination airport is the clearance limit, altitude is a separate element, and EFC belongs to holding instructions.

choices.text: The STAR name and the transition, if necessary.
The destination airport and the STAR name.
The STAR name and the assigned altitude.
The runway number and the STAR transition.

operations.problem: The correct answer ('An assigned altitude or an instruction to vertically navigate on the STAR or STAR transition.') is 88 characters while the distractors range from 21 to 29 characters. The 3–4x length differential makes the correct answer obvious without evaluating its content.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 4-7-1, block 4 (note), subparagraph c: 'Altitude instructions, as follows: 1. Assigned altitude; or 2. Instructions to vertically navigate on the STAR or STAR transition.'

match.question_text: Which altitude option may be part of an arrival clearance?

replacements.question_text: Which altitude option may be part of an arrival clearance?
replacements.explanation: Altitude instructions in an arrival clearance are either an assigned altitude or instructions to vertically navigate on the STAR or STAR transition. Holding altitudes with EFC times and missed-approach climb instructions belong to different clearance sections.

choices.text: An assigned altitude or instructions to vertically navigate on the STAR.
A single altitude crossing restriction at the IAF.
An EFC time with a holding altitude assignment.
A missed-approach climb gradient and altitude.

operations.problem: The true/false format presents a verbatim rule restatement as the stem and asks the learner to confirm it. This tests rule recognition rather than operational understanding. The explanation ('That is the stated runway-transition protection rule.') merely labels the rule without connecting it to the broader clearance framework. The same rule is tested more operationally by the situation_action activity (f1035acc), but a well-formed multiple-choice question on the 10 NM vector trigger is still warranted for recognition-level coverage.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 4-7-1, block 1 (phraseology): 'If the runway assignment, or any subsequent runway change, is not issued prior to 10 NM from the runway transition waypoint, radar vectors to final must be provided.'

match.question_text: If the runway assignment or a later runway change is not issued before 10 NM from the runway-transition waypoint, radar vectors to final must be provided.

replacements.question_text: When a STAR transition serves multiple runways and the runway assignment has not been issued before 10 NM from the runway-transition waypoint, what must the controller do?
replacements.explanation: If the runway assignment or any subsequent runway change is not issued prior to 10 NM from the runway-transition waypoint, radar vectors to final must be provided.

choices.text: Provide radar vectors to final.
Assign the nearest runway and continue the STAR.
Issue holding at the transition waypoint.
Cancel the STAR and issue a full route clearance.

operations.problem: The explanation ('The correct transmission is "ROSEWOOD ONE ARRIVAL, DELTA TRANSITION."') only echoes the assembled words without teaching the underlying phraseology template. A phraseology_builder activity should reinforce the standard pattern so the learner can generalize beyond this single example.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 4-7-1, block 2 (phraseology): '(STAR/RNAV STAR name and number) ARRIVAL, (transition name) TRANSITION.' Block 3 (example): 'Rosewood One arrival, Delta transition.'

content.situation: The aircraft is cleared via the Rosewood One arrival, Delta transition. Build the arrival phraseology.
content.explanation: The correct transmission is "ROSEWOOD ONE ARRIVAL, DELTA TRANSITION."

content.situation: The aircraft is cleared via the Rosewood One arrival, Delta transition. Build the arrival phraseology.
content.explanation: The standard phraseology template for a STAR clearance with transition follows the pattern: (STAR name and number) ARRIVAL, (transition name) TRANSITION. The clearance must include the name and transition, if necessary, of the STAR/RNAV STAR to be flown. For this situation the correct transmission is 'ROSEWOOD ONE ARRIVAL, DELTA TRANSITION.'

operations.problem: This knowledge_check activity duplicates question f12a9795 in the same retrieval mode. Both present an identical stem ('When a STAR or RNAV STAR is assigned, what must the clearance include?'), identical choices, and the same correct answer in a four-option multiple-choice format. The activity adds no new retrieval demand or reinforcement value.
operations.source_basis: Question f12a9795 covers the same source statement from paragraph 4-7-1, block 1.

content.question_text: When a STAR or RNAV STAR is assigned, what must the clearance include?

choices.text: The name of the STAR and the transition, if necessary.
Only the destination airport.
Only the runway number.
Only the EFC time.

content.explanation: When a STAR or RNAV STAR is assigned, the clearance includes the procedure name and transition if needed.

operations.problem: This list_membership activity duplicates question 1446c378 in the same retrieval mode. Both present an identical stem ('Which arrival transmission correctly includes both the STAR and transition?'), identical choices, and the same four-option multiple-choice structure. The list_membership type label does not change the fact that the cognitive task and content are identical to the existing question.
operations.source_basis: Question 1446c378 covers the same source example from paragraph 4-7-1, block 3.

content.question_text: Which arrival transmission correctly includes both the STAR and transition?

choices.text: Rosewood One arrival, Delta transition.
Proceed direct Delta, Rosewood only.
Arrival Rosewood, runway assigned later.
Delta transition, expect vectors at destination.

content.explanation: The approved phraseology states the STAR name and, when needed, the transition.

operations.problem: All three distractors begin with 'Only the…' and are substantially shorter than the correct answer, which includes a compound clause ('and, if appropriate, that airport weather is not available'). The structural and length disparity cues the correct answer independently of the student's knowledge of the rule.
operations.source_basis: 4-7-10b: 'Upon pilot request, controllers must inform pilots of the frequency where automated weather data may be obtained and, if appropriate, that airport weather is not available.'

match.question_text: Upon pilot request, what automated-weather information must controllers provide?

replacements.question_text: Upon pilot request, what automated-weather information must controllers provide?
replacements.explanation: Upon pilot request, controllers must inform pilots of the frequency where automated weather data may be obtained. If the airport has no automated weather broadcast capability, controllers must also inform the pilot that airport weather is not available.

choices.text: The frequency where automated weather may be obtained, and, if appropriate, that airport weather is not available.
The current altimeter setting, and, if applicable, the density altitude for the airport.
The latest METAR report, and, if available, the runway visual range for the landing runway.
The tower frequency for the destination, and, if required, the ATIS broadcast code.

operations.problem: The front 'What weather-related items are part of the normal approach-information package when they are not omitted?' is circular — the items are part of the package precisely when they are not omitted, so the question answers itself. The back uses 'threshold-triggered ceiling and visibility' which omits the specific thresholds from the source (below 1,000 feet/below highest circling minimum/visibility less than 3 miles). The card is also redundant with flashcard d909804f, which tests the same items 3-5 from the omission side. The card type 'definition' is incorrect for a list-of-items card. Replaced with a card covering paragraph d, an untested operational duty.
operations.source_basis: 4-7-10d: 'Advise pilots when the ILS on the runway in use is not operational if that ILS is on the same frequency as an operational ILS serving another runway.'

match.front: What weather-related items are part of the normal approach-information package when they are not omitted?
match.back: Surface wind, threshold-triggered ceiling and visibility, and the altimeter setting.

replacements.front: When must controllers advise pilots that the ILS for the runway in use is not operational?
replacements.back: When that ILS is on the same frequency as an operational ILS serving another runway.

operations.problem: The front has a grammatical error: 'When need special weather observations no longer be issued separately?' The missing auxiliary verb 'do' and inverted word order make the question awkward and harder to parse. The back is accurate.
operations.source_basis: 4-7-10c: 'Special weather observations need not be issued after they are included in the ATIS broadcast and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.'

match.front: When need special weather observations no longer be issued separately?
match.back: After they are included in ATIS and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

replacements.front: When do special weather observations no longer need to be issued separately?
replacements.back: After they are included in ATIS and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

operations.problem: The correct answer is a long verbatim quote from the source (24 words) while all distractors are short fragments (7-12 words), creating an obvious answer-length cue that reveals the correct choice regardless of knowledge. The stem is self-contained and tests the right concept.
operations.source_basis: Where the collocated or satellite tower has STARS data displayed on its CTRD, the STARS modify or quick look functions may be used to forward arrival data provided that a facility directive at the collocated tower or a letter of agreement with the satellite tower exists which outlines procedures for using STARS for transferring this data.

match.question_text: When may STARS modify or quick look functions be used to forward arrival data?

replacements.question_text: When may STARS modify or quick look functions be used to forward arrival data?
replacements.explanation: STARS modify or quick look functions may forward arrival data only when two conditions are both met: the tower has STARS data displayed on its CTRD, and a facility directive or letter of agreement exists outlining the procedures for using STARS to transfer arrival data. Visibility of STARS data alone does not authorize its use for forwarding.

choices.text: When the tower has STARS data on its CTRD and a facility directive or LOA outlines the transfer procedures.
Whenever STARS data is visible on the CTRD screen, regardless of governing documents.
Only at satellite towers with a letter of agreement in place.
Only after the local controller verbally confirms that conditions are adequate.

operations.problem: The correct answer is a long compound phrase (22 words) while distractors are short generic fragments (5-7 words), creating a strong answer-length cue. The distractors also fail to probe common operational confusions between the information forwarded to nonapproach towers versus collocated towers, despite these being the main learning distinction in the paragraph.
operations.source_basis: Forward the following information to the tower when the tower and TRACON are part of the same facility: 1. Aircraft identification. 2. Type aircraft if required for separation purposes. 3. Type of instrument approach procedure and/or runway if differing from that in use.

match.question_text: When the tower and TRACON are part of the same facility, what must be forwarded to the tower?

replacements.question_text: When the tower and TRACON are part of the same facility, what arrival information must be forwarded to the tower?
replacements.explanation: When the tower and TRACON are part of the same facility, approach control forwards three items: aircraft identification, aircraft type if required for separation purposes, and the type of instrument approach procedure or runway if differing from the one currently in use. This is a more concise list than the information forwarded to nonapproach control towers, which also includes ETA and does not condition aircraft type on separation needs.

choices.text: Aircraft identification, type if needed for separation, and approach or runway if differing from the one in use.
Aircraft identification, type of aircraft, and the estimated time of arrival.
Only the type of instrument approach procedure the aircraft will execute.
Aircraft identification and type only, without approach or runway information.

operations.problem: The correct answer is notably longer than the distractors, creating an answer-length cue. The stem phrase 'specifically forwarded' implies that only one detail is forwarded to centers, misleading the learner — seven categories of arrival information are forwarded to centers under subparagraph d.
operations.source_basis: Highest altitude being used by the approach control facility at the holding fix.

match.question_text: Which arrival detail is specifically forwarded from approach control to centers?

replacements.question_text: Which of the following is among the arrival details forwarded from approach control to centers?
replacements.explanation: Approach control forwards seven categories of arrival information to centers. Among them is the highest altitude being used by approach control at the holding fix. Other items forwarded to centers include the expected approach procedure when multiple approaches are published, average time interval between successive approaches, arrival time over the holding fix, revised EFC differing by 10 minutes or more, missed approaches affecting center operations, and information about unreported or overdue aircraft.

choices.text: Highest altitude used by approach control at the holding fix.
The estimated time of arrival of the arriving aircraft.
The type of instrument approach procedure the aircraft will execute.
The runway exit taxi instructions for the destination airport.

operations.problem: This knowledge check activity duplicates question b6cc4453 — same stem, same answer set, same retrieval mode — testing the collocated-tower forwarding list that is already covered by the question. The correct answer length creates a cue, and the explanation merely restates the answer without teaching the operational principle. Subparagraph 4-7-11a (nonapproach control tower and FSS forwarding) has no coverage in this shard; replacing this activity to cover it closes the gap.
operations.source_basis: Forward the following information to nonapproach control towers soon enough to permit adjustment of the traffic flow or to FSSs soon enough to provide local airport advisory where applicable: 1. Aircraft identification. 2. Type of aircraft. 3. ETA. 4. Type of instrument approach procedure the aircraft will execute; or 5. For SVFR, the direction from which the aircraft will enter Class B, Class C, Class D, or Class E surface area and any altitude restrictions that were issued; or 6. For aircraft executing a contact approach, the position of the aircraft.

content.question_text: When the tower and TRACON are part of the same facility, what arrival information must be forwarded to the tower?

choices.text: Aircraft identification, type if needed for separation, and the approach procedure and or runway if different from that in use.
Only the runway exit and gate assignment.
Only the aircraft's ETA.
Only the ATIS code and braking action.

content.explanation: Those are the required items forwarded to a collocated tower.

content.question_text: Which arrival information does approach control forward to nonapproach control towers or FSSs?

choices.text: Aircraft identification, type of aircraft, and ETA.
Aircraft identification and type only if required for separation purposes.
Highest altitude at the holding fix and average approach interval.
Only the type of instrument approach procedure to be executed.

content.explanation: Approach control forwards the following to nonapproach control towers, soon enough to permit traffic flow adjustment, and to FSSs, soon enough to provide local airport advisory: aircraft identification, type of aircraft, and ETA, plus the type of instrument approach procedure to be executed, or SVFR entry direction and altitude restrictions, or contact approach position — depending on the operation. This list differs from the collocated-tower list which omits ETA and adds the condition that aircraft type is forwarded only if required for separation.

operations.problem: This list membership activity duplicates question fbbc5c36, testing the same center-forwarded detail (highest altitude at holding fix) in a nearly identical multiple-choice format. The stem phrase 'specifically forwarded' implies exclusivity. Subparagraph 4-7-11d.1 (forwarding the expected approach procedure when multiple approaches are published) is an untested center item that tests a different operational skill than simple item identification.
operations.source_basis: Where two or more instrument approach procedures are published for the airport, the particular procedure which an aircraft can expect or that it will be vectored toward the airport for a visual approach.

content.question_text: Which arrival detail is specifically forwarded from approach control to centers?

choices.text: Highest altitude being used by the approach control facility at the holding fix.
Runway exit taxi instructions.
Field condition matrix only.
The airport's ATIS code letter.

content.explanation: The highest altitude being used at the holding fix is one of the items forwarded to centers.

content.instruction: Select the information forwarded to center.
content.question_text: When two or more instrument approach procedures are published for an airport, what does approach control forward to center?

choices.text: The approach procedure the aircraft can expect or be vectored toward.
The type of instrument approach procedure the aircraft will execute.
The highest altitude being used at the holding fix.
The runway currently in use at the destination.

content.explanation: When two or more instrument approach procedures are published for an airport, approach control forwards to center the particular procedure the aircraft can expect, or that it will be vectored toward the airport for a visual approach. This is distinct from the type of approach procedure forwarded to nonapproach control towers, which describes the procedure the aircraft will actually execute and is unconditional.

operations.problem: The explanation merely restates the correct answer without teaching the rule or its operational context. A requirement check explanation should help the learner understand why the statement is operationally correct and where this requirement fits among the broader set of center-forwarded information.
operations.source_basis: Revised EFC if different by 10 minutes or more from that issued by the center.

content.question_text: If the revised EFC differs by 10 minutes or more from the one issued by center, approach control must forward it to center.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: A revised EFC is forwarded when it differs by 10 minutes or more.

content.question_text: If the revised EFC differs by 10 minutes or more from the one issued by center, approach control must forward it to center.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: Approach control forwards a revised EFC to center when the new estimate differs by 10 minutes or more from the one originally issued by center. This requirement is one of seven categories of arrival information that approach control must forward to centers, along with items such as the approach procedure expected, highest holding altitude, average approach interval, and missed approach information.

operations.problem: The card back provides only the role title 'The local controller' without the operational context of what is being determined or under what authority. A flashcard back must contain enough substance for the learner to verify recall accuracy, especially for a definition card where the term's significance depends on context.
operations.source_basis: The local controller has the responsibility to determine whether or not conditions are adequate for the use of STARS data on the CTRD where a facility directive authorizes its use for the transfer of arrival data.

match.front: Who determines whether conditions are adequate for using STARS arrival data on the CTRD where authorized?

replacements.front: Who determines whether conditions are adequate for using STARS arrival data on the CTRD where a facility directive authorizes it?
replacements.back: The local controller has the responsibility to determine whether conditions are adequate for the use of STARS data on the CTRD where a facility directive authorizes its use for the transfer of arrival data.

operations.problem: The correct answer is a 17-word phrase while every distractor is a 5-8 word fragment beginning with 'Only...', creating both a length cue and a structural pattern that reveals the correct answer. The distractors are substantively valid (all test items explicitly excluded by the source), but their telegraphic 'Only...' form makes them implausible as controller-responsibility statements compared to the full-sentence correct answer.
operations.source_basis: Source 4-7-12a: 'Before issuing an approach clearance... inform an aircraft of any abnormal operation of approach and landing aids and of destination airport conditions that you know of which might restrict an approach or landing.' Source Note 1 excludes TAXIWAY/APRON/SERVICE NOTAMs, OBST NOTAMs, and cold-temperature compensation.

match.question_text: Before issuing an approach clearance, what known destination information must be provided?

replacements.question_text: Before issuing an approach clearance, what known destination information must be provided?
replacements.explanation: Controllers must inform aircraft of any known abnormal operation of approach or landing aids and any known airport conditions that might restrict the approach or landing before issuing an approach clearance, and subsequently as changes occur.

choices.text: Any abnormal operation of approach or landing aids and any airport condition that might restrict approach or landing.
Current tower frequency, runway configuration, and taxi assignments at the destination.
TAXIWAY, APRON, and SERVICE keyword NOTAMs for the destination airport.
Cold-temperature compensation information and general obstruction data.

operations.problem: The correct answer is a 25-word enumeration of all four RwyCC issuance points while every distractor is a 5-8 word fragment beginning with 'Only...'. The length and structural disparity cue the correct answer. The distractors are plausible operational mistakes but their telegraphic form undercuts them.
operations.source_basis: Source 4-7-12c: 'Issue RwyCC contained in a FICON NOTAM to aircraft in accordance with one of the following: 1. Before or when an approach clearance is issued. 2. Before an en route descent clearance is issued. 3. TERMINAL. Prior to departure. 4. As soon as possible after receipt of any subsequent changes in previously issued RwyCC information.'

match.question_text: When may RwyCC from a FICON NOTAM be issued?

replacements.question_text: When may RwyCC from a FICON NOTAM be issued?
replacements.explanation: RwyCC from a FICON NOTAM must be issued at specific operational points: before or with an approach clearance, before an en route descent clearance, prior to departure in terminal operations, and as soon as possible after any subsequent RwyCC change. RwyCC may be issued in lieu of the complete FICON NOTAM; the full NOTAM is issued only upon pilot request, workload permitting.

choices.text: Before or with approach clearance, before en route descent clearance, prior to terminal departure, and as soon as possible after RwyCC changes.
After the pilot specifically requests the complete FICON NOTAM.
After the aircraft has checked in with the destination tower.
When reported braking action is poor or nil at the destination.

operations.problem: This conditional_rule_check activity is a near-exact duplicate of question 5caa71f1 (true_false). Both items ask whether airport-condition information may be omitted when contained in ATIS and the pilot states the appropriate code, using the same true/false answer structure. The question format already covers this rule; the activity adds no new retrieval mode, depth, or operational context.
operations.source_basis: Source 4-7-12a/b: 'This information may be omitted if it is contained in the ATIS broadcast and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.' The question already validly assesses this rule.

content.question_text: If the restricting airport-condition information is already in ATIS and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code, you may omit that information.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: That information may be omitted when it is already in ATIS and the pilot states the correct code.

operations.problem: This knowledge_check activity is a near-exact duplicate of question 7bfbdd1a (multiple_choice). Both items ask what destination information must be provided before issuing an approach clearance, with the same correct answer and nearly identical distractors. The question format already covers this rule; the activity adds no distinct retrieval mode.
operations.source_basis: Source 4-7-12a: 'Before issuing an approach clearance... inform an aircraft of any abnormal operation of approach and landing aids and of destination airport conditions that you know of which might restrict an approach or landing.'

content.question_text: Before issuing an approach clearance, what destination information must be provided when known?

choices.text: Any abnormal operation of approach or landing aids and any airport condition that might restrict an approach or landing.
Only the current tower frequency and runway exit.
Only NOTAMs that concern taxiways or aprons.
Only cold-temperature compensation information in every case.

content.explanation: Approach and landing aid abnormalities and airport conditions that could restrict approach or landing must be issued when known.

operations.problem: This list_membership activity is a near-exact duplicate of question cb8ce7dc (multiple_choice). Both items ask which item is specifically excluded from en route airport-conditions information, with the same correct answer and identical distractors. The question format already covers this rule; the activity adds no distinct retrieval mode.
operations.source_basis: Source 4-7-12 Note 1(a): 'Airport conditions information, in the provision of en route approach control service, does not include the following: a. The airport surface environment other than the landing area(s) (e.g. TAXIWAY, APRON or SERVICE keyword NOTAMs).'

content.question_text: For en route airport-conditions information, which item is specifically excluded from the package?

choices.text: Taxiway, apron, or service-area NOTAMs outside the landing area.
Abnormal approach-aid operation that could restrict landing.
Destination airport conditions that might restrict an approach.
Runway condition code information.

content.explanation: Surface-environment items outside the landing area are excluded from en route airport-conditions information.

operations.problem: This requirement_check activity is a near-exact duplicate of question 397cab1c (multiple_choice). Both test the four issuance times for RwyCC from a FICON NOTAM. The activity's explanation ('Those are the listed times for issuing RwyCC information.') is thin and does not teach the operational principle. The question format covers this rule more robustly.
operations.source_basis: Source 4-7-12c(1-4): RwyCC must be issued before or with an approach clearance, before an en route descent clearance, prior to terminal departure, and as soon as possible after subsequent RwyCC changes.

content.question_text: RwyCC from a FICON NOTAM may be issued before or with the approach clearance, before an en route descent clearance, prior to departure in terminal operations, and as soon as possible after subsequent changes.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: Those are the listed times for issuing RwyCC information.

operations.problem: The front reads 'When may that airport-condition information be omitted?' — the word 'that' has no antecedent, making the card context-light. A learner seeing this card in isolation cannot determine which airport-condition information is being referenced without guessing or recalling surrounding context.
operations.source_basis: Source 4-7-12a/b: 'This information may be omitted if it is contained in the ATIS broadcast and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.' The 'information' refers to abnormal operation of approach/landing aids and destination airport conditions that might restrict an approach or landing.

match.front: When may that airport-condition information be omitted?
match.back: When it is contained in ATIS and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

replacements.front: When may airport-condition information that might restrict an approach or landing be omitted?
replacements.back: When it is contained in the ATIS broadcast and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

operations.problem: The back of this card is solely 'No.' — a single word that provides no operational principle. A flashcard back must teach the rule it tests. A learner who sees only 'No.' on review gains nothing and cannot reconstruct why the answer is no, defeating the purpose of spaced-repetition retrieval practice.
operations.source_basis: Source 4-7-12 Note 3: 'A pilot stating, or a controller asking, if they "have the NOTAMS" does not relieve the controller of the responsibility of issuing airport conditions that might restrict an approach or landing. Additionally, controller instructions to contact FSS to obtain the NOTAMs does not relieve the controller of their responsibilities specified in this paragraph.'

match.front: Does a pilot saying, "We have the NOTAMs," relieve ATC of the duty to issue restricting airport conditions?

replacements.front: Does a pilot saying, "We have the NOTAMs," relieve ATC of the duty to issue restricting airport conditions?
replacements.back: No. The controller retains the responsibility to issue known airport conditions that might restrict an approach or landing, regardless of the pilot's NOTAM status or instructions to contact FSS.

operations.problem: The correct answer ('Facilities using fixes formed by reference to the affected NAVAIDs') is a multi-word descriptive phrase, while all three distractors ('Only the destination tower,' 'Only the flight service station,' 'Only the airport manager') are short entity-only choices. The length and specificity disparity makes the correct answer visually identifiable without operational knowledge.
operations.source_basis: TERMINAL: When a change is made from one ILS to another at airports equipped with multiple systems which are not used simultaneously, coordinate with the facilities which use the fixes formed by reference to these NAVAIDs.

match.question_text: Who are the facilities that must be coordinated with during the ILS switch?

replacements.question_text: Which facilities must be coordinated with when switching from one ILS to another at an airport with multiple systems not used simultaneously?
replacements.explanation: Coordinate with facilities that use fixes formed by reference to the affected NAVAIDs.

choices.text: Facilities that use fixes formed by reference to the affected NAVAIDs.
Only the air traffic control tower that handles arrivals at the destination airport.
Only the terminal radar approach control serving the destination terminal area.
Only the facility that maintains the newly selected ILS equipment.

operations.problem: The explanation ('Those are the facilities specifically named in the rule') is thin—it merely gestures at the answer rather than teaching the operating requirement—and uses document-scaffolding language by referencing what is 'named in the rule.'
operations.source_basis: TERMINAL: When a change is made from one ILS to another at airports equipped with multiple systems which are not used simultaneously, coordinate with the facilities which use the fixes formed by reference to these NAVAIDs.

match.question_text: Coordination must be accomplished with facilities that use fixes formed by reference to the affected NAVAIDs.

replacements.question_text: Coordination must be accomplished with facilities that use fixes formed by reference to the affected NAVAIDs.
replacements.explanation: When switching from one ILS to another at an airport with multiple systems not used simultaneously, coordinate with facilities that use fixes formed by reference to those NAVAIDs.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The activity type 'list_membership' does not match the task, which asks for a single correct answer rather than identifying which items belong to a defined set. The correct answer is substantially longer than the three short distractors, creating an answer-length cue. The explanation ('Those are the facilities named in the coordination requirement') uses document-scaffolding language and does not teach the operating requirement.
operations.source_basis: TERMINAL: When a change is made from one ILS to another at airports equipped with multiple systems which are not used simultaneously, coordinate with the facilities which use the fixes formed by reference to these NAVAIDs.

content.question_text: Which facilities are the ones that must be coordinated with in this ILS switch?

choices.text: Facilities using fixes formed by reference to the affected NAVAIDs.
Only facilities using the departure runway.
Only the flight service station.
Only the airport manager.

content.question_text: Which facilities must be coordinated with when switching from one ILS to another at an airport with multiple systems not used simultaneously?

choices.text: Facilities that use fixes formed by reference to the affected NAVAIDs.
Only the control tower that handles arrivals at the destination airport.
Only the terminal approach control responsible for the destination airspace.
Only the facility that operates the newly selected ILS equipment.

content.explanation: When switching from one ILS to another at an airport with multiple systems not used simultaneously, coordinate with facilities that use fixes formed by reference to those NAVAIDs.

operations.problem: The explanation ('That is the direct coordination requirement in the paragraph') uses document-scaffolding language ('in the paragraph') and does not state the operating requirement itself. The statement being evaluated and the True/False choices are correct.
operations.source_basis: TERMINAL: When a change is made from one ILS to another at airports equipped with multiple systems which are not used simultaneously, coordinate with the facilities which use the fixes formed by reference to these NAVAIDs.

content.question_text: When changing from one ILS to another at an airport with multiple systems not used simultaneously, coordinate with facilities that use the fixes formed by reference to those NAVAIDs.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: That is the direct coordination requirement in the paragraph.

content.question_text: When changing from one ILS to another at an airport with multiple systems not used simultaneously, coordinate with facilities that use the fixes formed by reference to those NAVAIDs.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: When switching from one ILS to another at an airport with multiple systems not used simultaneously, coordinate with facilities that use fixes formed by reference to those NAVAIDs.

operations.problem: The front asks 'Why is this coordination required?' but the source states only the directive to coordinate; it does not provide a reason or rationale. The back ('Because other facilities are using fixes formed by reference to the NAVAIDs being switched') infers a causal explanation that the source does not make explicit. The card_type 'definition' also does not match the content, which attempts to elicit a rationale.
operations.source_basis: TERMINAL: When a change is made from one ILS to another at airports equipped with multiple systems which are not used simultaneously, coordinate with the facilities which use the fixes formed by reference to these NAVAIDs.

match.front: Why is this coordination required?
match.back: Because other facilities are using fixes formed by reference to the NAVAIDs being switched.

replacements.front: At what type of airport does the ILS switch coordination requirement apply?
replacements.back: Airports equipped with multiple ILS systems that are not used simultaneously.

operations.problem: The correct answer ('An aircraft landing at an airport in an adjacent center's area near the common boundary') is a multi-word operational description, while all three distractors ('Any aircraft requesting a cruise clearance,' 'Only VFR aircraft approaching a Class D airport,' 'Only aircraft already on a published approach procedure') are short generic phrases. The length and specificity disparity makes the correct answer identifiable without operational knowledge.
operations.source_basis: EN ROUTE: Take the following action when exercising control of aircraft landing at an airport located in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary.

match.question_text: Which aircraft is covered by this advance descent rule?

replacements.question_text: Which aircraft are covered by the advance descent clearance rule?
replacements.explanation: The advance descent clearance rule applies when controlling aircraft landing at an airport located in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary.

choices.text: Aircraft landing at an airport in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary.
Aircraft on an instrument approach to any airport within the same center's control area.
Aircraft receiving radar vectors to the primary airport in the adjacent center's airspace.
Aircraft requesting a visual approach at an airport in the overlying center's airspace.

operations.problem: The explanation ('That is the stated timing standard') is thin—it characterizes the statement without teaching the operating requirement. The statement being evaluated and the True/False choices are correct.
operations.source_basis: EN ROUTE: Initiate this action at a distance sufficient from destination to allow for normal descent and speed reduction.

match.question_text: Advance descent action should be initiated far enough from destination to allow normal descent and speed reduction.

replacements.question_text: Advance descent action should be initiated far enough from destination to allow normal descent and speed reduction.
replacements.explanation: Initiate advance descent action at a distance sufficient from the destination to allow for normal descent and speed reduction.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The front ('Which aircraft does this rule cover?') relies on the generic reference 'this rule,' which does not identify the concept being recalled without the surrounding deck context. The back is correct.
operations.source_basis: EN ROUTE: Take the following action when exercising control of aircraft landing at an airport located in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary.

match.front: Which aircraft does this rule cover?
match.back: Aircraft landing at an airport located in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary.

replacements.front: Which aircraft triggers the advance descent clearance requirement?
replacements.back: An aircraft landing at an airport located in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary.

operations.problem: The back of this flashcard says only 'Yes.' The front asks a substantive question about whether a frequency change may be required when the pilot cancels IFR during SFA. A one-word back provides no retrieval substance — the student sees they answered correctly but receives no reinforcement of why. The back must state the operative exception that makes the answer 'yes.'
operations.source_basis: Source block 1 lists 'When pilot cancels IFR flight plan' as exception (c) to the no-frequency-change rule. The flashcard must retrieve that exception, not merely confirm correctness.

match.front: If the pilot cancels IFR during SFA, may a frequency change be required?

replacements.front: If the pilot cancels IFR during an SFA procedure, may a frequency change be required?
replacements.back: Yes — pilot IFR cancellation is a listed exception to the no-frequency-change rule.

operations.problem: The front asks the student to 'Name one listed exception' but the back enumerates all five exceptions. This mismatch undermines flashcard coherence: the student cannot verify recall of a single exception against a full list without seeing every other exception. The front should ask for the complete list, which is the intended retrieval target.
operations.source_basis: Source block 1 enumerates five distinct exceptions (a through e) to the no-frequency-change rule. The full set of exceptions is the operational retrieval target — a controller must know all conditions under which a frequency change may be required.

match.front: Name one listed exception to the SFA no-frequency-change rule.
match.back: Daylight VFR conditions, pilot request, IFR cancellation, an emergency, or a visual approach.

replacements.front: What are the listed exceptions to the SFA no-frequency-change rule?
replacements.back: During daylight VFR conditions, on pilot request, when the pilot cancels IFR, in an emergency situation, or when the aircraft is cleared for a visual approach.

operations.problem: The explanation states only 'That is the core SFA restriction on radio-frequency changes.' It does not teach the operating requirement or identify the listed exceptions. The student receives no substantive reinforcement of the rule after answering.

content.explanation: Under an SFA procedure, do not require a radio frequency change after the aircraft begins approach or after initial contact during an en route descent until a landing or low approach has been completed. The listed exceptions are: daylight VFR conditions, pilot request, IFR cancellation, emergency, and visual approach clearance.

operations.problem: The explanation uses the word 'minimize' to describe the rule: 'SFA procedures minimize frequency changes after approach begins or after initial contact during en route descent until landing or low approach completion.' The source block uses 'do not require' — a direct prohibition with specific exceptions, not a minimization goal. 'Minimize' softens the operational requirement and implies discretion the rule does not grant.
operations.source_basis: Source block 0 states 'do not require a radio frequency change after the aircraft begins approach or after initial contact during an en route descent until a landing or low approach has been completed except under the following conditions.' The construal is a prohibition, not a preference.

operations.problem: The back uses 'such as' to introduce the four conditions for a nonradar tower-frequency change, which misrepresents the definitive source list as illustrative examples rather than the complete set of operative conditions.
operations.source_basis: Source sub-item e: 'When a nonradar approach will be made, aircraft may be instructed to change to tower frequency when: 1. The reported ceiling is at or above 1,500 feet and visibility is 5 statute miles or more. 2. The aircraft reports able to proceed by visual reference to the surface. 3. The aircraft requests and is cleared for a contact approach. 4. The aircraft is cleared for a visual approach.'

match.front: When may a change to tower frequency be allowed on a nonradar approach?
match.back: When one of the listed conditions exists, such as at least 1,500-foot ceiling and 5-mile visibility, pilot visual reference to the surface, contact approach, or visual approach.

replacements.front: When may a change to tower frequency be allowed on a nonradar approach?
replacements.back: When: ceiling is at or above 1,500 feet and visibility is 5 SM or more; the aircraft reports able to proceed by visual reference to the surface; the aircraft requests and is cleared for a contact approach; or the aircraft is cleared for a visual approach.

operations.source_basis: Para 4-7-6 note block 2: '1. Aircraft identification. 2. Type of aircraft and appropriate aircraft equipment suffix. 3. Clearance limit (when other than the destination airport) and EFC issued to the aircraft. Clearance limit may be omitted when provided for in a letter of agreement. 5. Time, fix, or altitude when control responsibility is transferred to the approach control facility. This information may be omitted when provided for in a letter of agreement.'

False

content.explanation: When a weather report is relayed through a communications station other than an air carrier company radio or through a nonapproach control facility, the controller may direct that station or facility to issue current weather to the arriving aircraft. This satisfies the en route weather transmission requirement.

operations.problem: The front asks 'What kind of weather changes are also transmitted under this rule?' The phrase 'under this rule' has no antecedent and does not identify the concept being recalled.
operations.source_basis: Transmit the weather report and changes classified as special weather observations to an arriving aircraft prior to or as part of the approach clearance.

match.front: What kind of weather changes are also transmitted under this rule?
match.back: Changes classified as special weather observations.

replacements.front: When en route weather below thresholds is transmitted to an arriving aircraft, what type of observations must be included along with the standard weather report?
replacements.back: Changes classified as special weather observations.

operations.problem: The front asks 'May a nonapproach control facility be instructed to issue current weather to satisfy this rule?' The phrase 'this rule' has no antecedent. The back is a bare 'Yes.' with no substantive content, making it useless as a retrieval target.
operations.source_basis: It is relayed through a communications station other than an air carrier company radio or through a nonapproach control facility. You may do this by telling the station or nonapproach control facility to issue current weather.

match.front: May a nonapproach control facility be instructed to issue current weather to satisfy this rule?

replacements.front: Under the en route weather transmission requirement, may a controller instruct a nonapproach control facility to issue current weather to an arriving aircraft?
replacements.back: Yes. A nonapproach control facility may be instructed to issue current weather when the weather report cannot be transmitted directly to the pilot. This is an authorized relay method.
